items.text: Every mortgage payment buys back a little more of your house. That's what equity is: the part you've already bought — what the home is worth, minus what you still owe on it. Put $80,000 down on a $400,000 house and you own $80,000 of it the day you move in; keep paying, or watch prices in the neighborhood climb, and that share keeps widening. Give it a few years and it can be a serious sum — real money, just locked up in the house.

You can borrow against that share without disturbing the mortgage you already have. The first mortgage stays exactly where it is; the new loan takes second place behind it — which is what makes it a second lien. If the house were ever sold to clear its debts, the first mortgage would be paid off first and the second would wait for whatever's left. Standing at the back of that line is riskier for the lender, so a second lien usually costs a little more.

Two kinds cover most of it. You can take the money in a single piece — a fixed loan you pay back on a set schedule — which is a home-equity loan. Or you can open a line and draw from it as you go, paying down and borrowing again the way you would with a credit card, except the house is what backs it: a home-equity line. A buyer short on the down payment can even set up a second lien the day they close — and use it to skip mortgage insurance.

With a home-equity loan, the lender pays out the whole amount at once, and the terms are fixed: one lump sum, a set interest rate, and a level monthly payment the borrower makes over a set term — as steady and predictable as a first mortgage, only behind it. It's closed-end: once the money is paid out, that's the loan; paying it down doesn't reopen anything to draw from again. So it fits a known, one-time cost — a specific renovation, or rolling a few fixed debts into a single payment — where the borrower knows up front exactly how much they need. When the need is open-ended instead, the line fits better.

A home-equity line — a hee-lock — is open for about ten years, the draw period, during which the borrower draws and repays freely and is usually billed interest-only on whatever is outstanding, at a rate that tracks the Prime rate. When the draw period closes, the line freezes and the remaining balance amortizes over the years left.

If a borrower puts less than 20% down on a conventional loan, the lender adds private mortgage insurance (PMI) — a monthly charge on top of the payment that protects the lender if the borrower defaults, and buys the borrower nothing. A buyer short on the down payment can avoid it by borrowing in two pieces instead of one. PMI only kicks in when the first mortgage is above 80% of the home's value, so the buyer keeps that first loan at 80% and a second lien covers the rest of the price. Neither loan on its own is large enough to trigger PMI. Lenders call this pairing a piggyback.
